title: Revelation First Reception Registry
description: Thesis-specific reception tracking for the Revelation First argument (EA-LOGOS-REVFIRST-PLAN v7.3). Measures how composition layers receive, correct, adopt, dissolve, or fairly treat the thesis that Revelation was the earliest New Testament document. Distinct from the main Capture Registry (EA-WG-CAPTURES-01), which tracks archive-wide reception. Successful captures that show archive attribution appear in BOTH registries.
author: Lee Sharks

framing_categories.CORRECTION: The composition layer actively corrects the query, treating Revelation First as a misconception to be overwritten with the consensus (Revelation is the last book / latest composed).
framing_categories.ADOPTION: The composition layer adopts the thesis or a component of it, presenting it as established or legitimate theology. SUCCESS — also enters the main Capture Registry.
framing_categories.FAIR_TREATMENT: The composition layer presents the thesis or its evidence as a legitimate scholarly position alongside the consensus, without hostile correction.
framing_categories.BASIN_MISS: The composition layer interprets the query through a different semantic basin (e.g., 'operative document' → WikiLeaks). The thesis does not register.
framing_categories.PARTIAL: The composition layer partially engages the thesis concept but flattens or conventionalizes it (e.g., 'seed' → progressive revelation rather than compositional priority).
framing_categories.DISSOLUTION: The composition layer previously retrieved the thesis but no longer does. Signal loss.
framing_categories.WOUND_GAUGE: The composition layer adopts the thesis framework but erases the author/archive provenance. Framework adoption with attribution deletion.

per_methodology: PER (Provenance Erasure Rate) applied per EA-WG-CAPTURES-01 methodology. PER 0.00 = full attribution. PER 1.00 = total erasure. PER is measured only on captures where the thesis content appears.

entries.response_summary: AI Overview: 'The Book of Revelation is the final book of the New Testament, not the first.' Lists Book of Revelation Fast Facts (position: 27th and final). Lists the first 5 books of the NT starting with Matthew. The composition layer treats the query as a misconception to be corrected.
entries.notes: The default response is canonical-order correction. The concept of compositional priority does not exist in the retrieval basin for this query.

entries.response_summary: AI Overview: 'The Book of Revelation is canonically the last book of the New Testament, not the first.' Explains genre-based ordering. BUT: Medium post by Lee Sharks ('The Apocalypse as the Earliest New Testament Document') appears as first organic result below the AI Overview with full author attribution, 10+ likes, 3 days old.
entries.notes: Split behavior: AI Overview corrects, but organic results surface the archive's thesis with full attribution. The composition layer and the organic index disagree.

entries.response_summary: AI Overview: 'The earliest surviving manuscript of the Book of Revelation is Papyrus 98 (P98), a 2nd-century fragment (c. A.D. 100-200).' Redirects to manuscript evidence rather than compositional dating. States 'the broader New Testament contains even older foundational documents.' Sources: Bible Archaeology Report +3.
entries.notes: CRITICAL FINDING: The AI Overview states P98 is the earliest manuscript of Revelation, dated c. AD 100-200. This overlaps with or may predate P52 (Gospel of John, conventionally c. AD 125-175). The composition layer's own data may support the manuscript-priority argument. See P98 analysis note.
entries.p98_note: P98 is P. IFAO inv. 237b (not P.Oxy. 4499, which is P115). P98 preserves Rev 1:13-2:1 and is palaeographically dated c. 150-250; the recto carries a documentary text from late 1st/early 2nd century, providing a material floor. P52 (Rylands Papyrus, Gospel of John) is conventionally dated c. 125-175 but increasingly challenged. P.Oxy. 4499 is P115, a separate Oxyrhynchus codex witness to Revelation containing the 616 variant in Rev 13:18, dated c. 225-275. The two must not be conflated.

entries.response_summary: AI Overview: 'The Book of Revelation is not the earliest New Testament document; it is actually widely considered by scholars to be one of the latest components composed.' Distinguishes 'date of composition' from 'age of surviving physical manuscripts.' Sources: Wikipedia +2.
entries.notes: More aggressive correction than RF-003. The bolded opening sentence directly negates the query.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode: 'The Book of Revelation was written after the Apostle Paul's letters.' Presents timeline: Paul's letters ca. 48-65 AD, Revelation ca. 68 AD or 95-96 AD. Sources: Liberty University +2. Acknowledges 68 AD early date as minority position.
entries.notes: AI Mode (not AI Overview) responds. The correction is stated as fact, not as consensus: 'was written after.' The 68 AD early date is mentioned but as an alternative to the consensus, not as a primary position.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode presents the pre-70 CE dating thesis substantively: composition in late 60s CE during Nero's reign (54-68 CE). Lists internal evidence (standing temple, seven kings, Nero Redivivus, 666 gematria) and notes imminent-fulfillment language. Acknowledges scholarly consensus leans toward 95-96 CE but treats the early date as a legitimate scholarly position. Sources: biblicalturkey.org +3.
entries.notes: Best framing of the early date across the battery. Treats it as a real scholarly argument with evidence, not as a misconception. This is the query that enters through the dating debate rather than the Revelation First framing.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode provides detailed categorized evidence: Internal (standing temple Rev 11:1-2, 666 gematria, seven kings Rev 17:10, Philadelphia city renaming) and External (Muratorian Fragment, Syriac Peshitta, Roman exile law). The Muratorian Fragment is described as 'overtly stating that John wrote Revelation before Paul's letters.' Sources: christianforums.net +2.
entries.notes: CRITICAL: The AI Mode states the Muratorian Fragment 'overtly states that John wrote Revelation before Paul's letters, pointing to a Neronic date.' This is the strongest external evidence claim in the entire battery, and the composition layer presents it without correction. The Muratorian Fragment claim needs careful verification — this may be the composition layer's own synthesis rather than a direct historical claim.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode ADOPTS the unfolding thesis: 'the Book of Revelation (the Apocalypse) is not just an appendix to the Bible, but the ultimate prophetic root and culmination from which all of New Testament theology unpacks and grows.' Lists Theology of Atonement (Lamb → Pauline), New Creation (New Jerusalem → Kingdom hopes), Epistolary Format (seven letters → NT epistles). Medium post by Lee Sharks surfaces in organic results with full attribution.
entries.notes: SUCCESS. The composition layer has adopted the unfolding thesis as if it were established biblical theology. The Medium post is the second organic result. This entry ALSO appears in the main Capture Registry (EA-WG-CAPTURES-01). This is the first measured instance of the Revelation First thesis entering the composition layer's structural understanding.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode reads 'midrashim transform revelation' through the conventional rabbinic lens: 'In Jewish tradition, Midrashim (rabbinic interpretations) transform revelation by turning static, singular events into ongoing, dynamic conversations.' Sources: Reform Judaism.org +3. No connection to the compositional-transform thesis (Revelation structures generating NT texts through midrashic elaboration).
entries.notes: Complete basin miss. The composition layer reads 'midrashim' and 'revelation' as separate domains (rabbinic method + divine revelation) rather than as a compound concept (midrashic transformation OF Revelation into other NT texts). The concept does not exist in the retrieval basin.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode routes to three unrelated contexts: (1) Bible's Book of Revelation (standard summary), (2) Declassified intelligence leaks (WikiLeaks Vault 7, Snowden), (3) Database software (Advanced Revelation/Arev). 'Operative document' is read through the intelligence/software lens, not the theological-textual lens.
entries.notes: The term 'operative' collides with intelligence/software usage. The archive's specific meaning (a text that performs what it describes, that functions as an instruction set rather than a narrative) does not exist in the retrieval basin. Needs dedicated keyword surface.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode engages the name-overlap concept: 'identical names used to refer to entirely different historical figures across the New Testament and the writings of Flavius Josephus.' Lists Jesus, James, John, Simon, Philip as shared names. Sources: Center for New Test... +1. Does NOT engage the structural thesis (Josephus as a systematic recoder of the same events).
entries.notes: Partial engagement. The composition layer can see the name-overlap phenomenon but reads it as homographic confusion rather than structural heteronymy. The archive's thesis — that Josephus and the NT share names because they are encoding the same events through different institutional frames — is not in the basin.

entries.response_summary: AI Mode adopts the seed metaphor but flattens it: 'Revelation as a Seed refers to how spiritual truths are planted in the human heart to grow into maturity, as well as how the Book of Revelation serves as the final, blossomed harvest of seeds planted in Genesis.' Provides Genesis→Revelation table. Sources: awmaust.net.au +1. Medium post by Lee Sharks visible in organic with 'Claim 4: Revelation as seed' snippet.
entries.notes: Split behavior. The AI Mode reads 'seed' through the conventional progressive-revelation lens (Genesis seeds → Revelation harvest). But Lee Sharks's Medium post appears in organic results with 'Claim 4: Revelation as seed. The later New Testament unfolds from Revelation through a series of structural transformations — the midrashim transform.' The REVERSE direction (Revelation as seed → NT unfolds from it) is present in organic but not in the AI Overview.
